coredata.dc:title: Spectrofluorimetric determination of Cr(VI) and Cr(III) by quenching effect of Cr(III) based on the Cu-CDs with peroxidase-mimicking activity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Qiulan
Yang, Dezhi
Yang, Yaling
coredata.dc:description: The study presents a spectrofluorimetric method for the determination of Cr(III) based on 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) as fluorescent nano sensor. With the peroxidase-mimicking activity of copper-doped carbon dots (Cu-CDs), colorless o-phenylenediamine (OPD) was oxidized to fluorescent DAP in the presence of H2O2 via generation of hydroxyl radicals. The Cr(III) was found to decrease the fluorescent intensity of the Cu-CDs-mediated OPD oxidation system. Cr(VI) species were reduced to Cr(III) by employing 2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonate) (ABTS) as the reductant, and a novel spectrofluorimetric method for the highly sensitive detection of speciation Cr(VI) and Cr(III) was developed. The Cu-CDs nanozyme was prepared from ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid copper disodium salt (Na2[Cu (EDTA)]) by one-step pyrolysis method. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of DAP is linearly proportional to the Cr(III) ion concentration in the range of 5 × 10−6 to 1.5 × 10−4 mol L−1 and the detection limit is 1.2 × 10−7 mol L−1. The method has a good anti-interference performance against some metal ions, such as, Hg2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, K+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Al3+, and Fe3+. The proposed method was successfully applied in the determination of Cr(VI) and Cr(III) in water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 2,3-Diaminophenazine
Copper doped carbon dots
Cr(III)
Cr(VI)
2,2′-Azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonate)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent detection of Cr(III) and Cr(VI)
Catalytic oxidation properties of Cu-CDs and Michaelis equations
Sensitivity and selectivity of the method

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Kunming University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was strongly supported by Kunming University of Science and Technology .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of spectrofluorimetric determination of Cr(VI) and Cr(III) based on Cu-CDs peroxidase-mimicking activity.

Characterizations of the prepared Cu-CDs; (a) full survey XPS spectrum; (b) high-resolution XPS spectrum of the Cu-CDs in the Cu2p region; (c) FTIR spectrum of Cu-CDs; (d) FTIR spectrum of Cu-CDs; (e) TEM image of Cu-CDs (270 °C).
Effect of (a) the reaction temperature and (b) pH. Under the same concentration condition for: [H2O2]: 1.25 × 10−3 mol L−1, [OPD]: 2.5 × 10−2 mol L−1.
The relationship between reaction velocities and OPD concentration ([H2O2] = 1.25 × 10−3 mol L−1, pH = 7 and 37 °C water bath).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of OPD oxidation system with the addition of different concentrations of Cr(III) (0.00–1.5 × 10−4 mol L−1); (b) linear curve for Cr(III) ([OPD] = 5 × 10−3 mol L−1, [H2O2] = 1.25 × 10−3 mol L−1, pH = 7 and temperature at 37 °C).
Fluorescence response of OPD oxidation system in the presence of (a) different metal ions, (b) other metal ions and Cr(III) and (c) shelter the Cu2+.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cr(III) (A) and excitation (B) and emission spectrum (C) of DAP; (b) fluorescence decay of DAP in the absence and presence of Cr(III).
The analysis results of certified reference materials (n = 3).
Determination of Cr(III) and Cr(VI) in water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Spectrofluorimetric determination of Cr(VI) and Cr(III) by quenching effect of Cr(III) based on the Cu-CDs with peroxidase-mimicking activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, Yunnan 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, Yunnan, 650500, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The study presents a spectrofluorimetric method for the determination of Cr(III) based on 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) as fluorescent nano sensor. With the peroxidase-mimicking activity of copper-doped carbon dots (Cu-CDs), colorless o-phenylenediamine (OPD) was oxidized to fluorescent DAP in the presence of H2O2 via generation of hydroxyl radicals. The Cr(III) was found to decrease the fluorescent intensity of the Cu-CDs-mediated OPD oxidation system. Cr(VI) species were reduced to Cr(III) by employing 2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonate) (ABTS) as the reductant, and a novel spectrofluorimetric method for the highly sensitive detection of speciation Cr(VI) and Cr(III) was developed. The Cu-CDs nanozyme was prepared from ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid copper disodium salt (Na2[Cu (EDTA)]) by one-step pyrolysis method. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of DAP is linearly proportional to the Cr(III) ion concentration in the range of 5 × 10−6 to 1.5 × 10−4 mol L−1 and the detection limit is 1.2 × 10−7 mol L−1. The method has a good anti-interference performance against some metal ions, such as, Hg2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, K+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Al3+, and Fe3+. The proposed method was successfully applied in the determination of Cr(VI) and Cr(III) in water samples.

Cu-CDs have been employed as a peroxidase-mimicking nanozyme for the oxidation of o-phenylenediamine to 2,3-diaminophenazine. The fluorescence of products could be quenched by Cr(III).
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of industry, heavy metals contamination in aqueous environment has attracted growing attention. Chromium (Cr) is the second most abundant inorganic groundwater contaminant at hazardous waste sites [1], which is mainly used in leather preparations, industrial pigments, tanned leather and metal parts [2]. As a transition metal, Cr has principal as the presence form of trivalent Cr(III) and hexavalent Cr(VI) in natural aqueous environment [3]. The toxicity of Cr is mainly related to its valence state [4,5]. The toxicity of Cr(III) is much lower than Cr(VI) [6]. Conversely, Cr(III) is a fundamental trace element in human bodies. As a significant glucose tolerance factor Cr(III) plays an important role in glucose utilization [7,8]. However, high concentrations Cr(III) can bind to DNA, leading to the damage of cellular structures and components [9]. Hence, a simple and reliable detection for the concentration of Cr is very necessary. The speciation of Cr was sensitively analyzed with techniques such as inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [10,11], atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [12,13], electrochemistry [14,15]. These methods are not widely applied in process of industry due to their complicated sample manipulation processes, time-consuming analysis and expensive equipment. Spectrofluorimetric method remedies these defects of methods the mentioned above, and saves time and cost.

By virtue of the low toxicity, convenient raw materials, simple synthetic routes, high quantum yield [16–19], and excellent electron acceptors and electron donors [20], carbon dots (CDs) have become the most investigated carbon nano materials family in the past few decades. Particularly, because CDs usually have broadband light-absorption ability and superior electron transfer ability [21], a series of photocatalysis basing on CDs have been developed in recent years [22,23]. Therefore, CDs showed the great potential in catalysis chemistry. Nevertheless, the fast recombination rate of the photoexcited electrons carriers leads to its limited quantum efficient [24]. At present, some excellent researchers focused on CDs-based composites for enhancement of catalytic ability of CDs. Copper (Cu) has attracted a great deal of concern due to its superior conductivity, higher stability, high availability and low cost [25].

Application of fluorescent materials in the sensitive detection of metal ions has tremendous potential [26]. DAP have caught many eyeballs due to luminance strongly both in aqueous solutions and in polar organic solvents [27]. There are numerous determination methods of organic and inorganic substance based on the fluorescence quenching of highly fluorescent product have been developed. Yong Bi-Rong et al. reported a high selectivity and sensitivity detection method of cyanide based on DAP [28]; P. Borthakur et al. developed a detection probe for Cr(VI) based on CuS nanoparticles [29]; Fang et al. presented a simple and fast fluorescent method coupled with DAP have been developed for the detection of dopamine [30]; Tang N. L et al. reported a new method based on 2,3-diaminophenazine with strong fluorescence for the determination of hydrogen peroxide [31]; Nevertheless, there are few reported that DAP utilized as a fluorescent probe for the determination of Cr(III). In addition, to the best of our knowledge, there are many theories about mechanisms of metal ions on the fluorescence quenching have been reported yet. Numerous researchers ascribed the fluorescence quenching mechanism to the inner filter effect (IFE) [32] and the electron, charge or energy transfer between CDs and metal ions [33]. In particular, as a major detection mechanism, IFE was widely applied on the fields of chemical sensing [34,35].

The present paper proposes a new fluorescence analysis method for determining Cr(III) and Cr(VI). The fluorescent DAP was produced by the oxidation of colorless OPD in the presence of H2O2 via generation of hydroxyl radicals with Cu-CDs as catalyst. Additionally, the fluorescence of DAP was effectively quenched by Cr(III) due to IFE. These Cu-CDs were synthesized from Na2[Cu(EDTA)] by carrying out one-step pyrolysis method, well characterized and then used in the OPD catalytic oxidation. Based on that, a selective IFE-based fluorescence sensor for the detection of Cr(III) was proposed. Cr(VI) was reduced to Cr(III) by employing ABTS as the reductant and could be quantitatively detected. Cr(III) and Cr(VI) in environmental samples were successfully measured by using the method (Fig. 1 ).

Na2[Cu(EDTA)] and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt were purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). o-Phenylenediamine and ABTS were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7), sodium hydroxide, hydrochloric acid, chromic nitrate (Cr(NO3)3), sodium diethyldithiocarbamatre (DDTC) and 30% H2O2 were purchased by Shantou Xi Long Chemical Factory Co. Ltd. The reagents of different metal ions Hg2+ (Hg(NO3)2), Co2+ (CoCl2), Cu2+ (CuSO4), Mg2+ (MgCl2), Ba2+ (BaSO4), K+ (KNO3), Mn2+ (MnCl2), Ni2+ (Ni(NO3)2), Al3+ (Al(NO3)3), and Fe3+ (FeCl3) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich.
All chemicals utilized were of analytical reagent and deionized water (18.2 MΩ cm) was employed for the preparation of all solutions.

The Cu-CDs were prepared through one-step pyrolysis which has been reported by Wu's group [36]. The preparation was accomplished on a high temperature tube furnace (Shanghai Sager Furnace Co. Ltd.). A quartz sample boat filled with 1 g Na2[Cu(EDTA)] was put into the middle of the quart tube, and calcined in the tube furnace under an N2 atmosphere at 270 °C for 2 h. Afterward, the tube furnace was naturally cooled down to room temperature. The collected target product was grinded, transferred and dissolved in a sample bottle with water (2 mg ml−1). And the product was processed by the ultrasonic treatment at room temperature for 20 min. The resulting solution was stored at 4 °C for further studies.

The morphology and microstructure of the Cu-CDs were observed on a TecnaiG2 TF30 transmission electron microscope (TEM) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The chemical states of the Cu-CDs were investigated by an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) equipped with Al X-ray source analysis. Further evidence for the composition of the product was performed on a Bruker FTIR spectrometer interfaced to the Hyperion microscope, which was equipped with a MCT (HgCdTe) detector cooled with liquid nitrogen. Moreover, the FTIR spectrum of CDs without Cu doped was performed. Fluorescence spectra of Cu-CDs were measured using Agilent Technologies' CaryEclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer. UV–Vis absorption spectra were determined on a TU-1901 Double beam UV–visible spectrophotometer from Beijing General Analysis Instrument co. Ltd.

The Cu-CDs were employed as the peroxidase-mimicking for efficiently catalyzed the oxidation reaction of OPD with the aim of promoting the production of high fluorescent DAP. 0.3 mL of 5 × 10−3 mol L−1 OPD solution and 0.1 mL H2O2 (5 × 10−2 mol L−1) and 0.1 mL of 2 mg mL−1 Cu-CDs solution were put into a test-tube, and then diluted with deionized water to 4 mL. After the mixed solution was vortex-mixed for 30 s, the reaction solution was placed in 37 °C water baths for 25 min. The fluorescence intensity of reaction solution was measured under excitation at 421 nm.

In order to promote the fluorescence yield of DAP, the kinetics of the oxidation reaction between OPD and H2O2 in presence of Cu-CDs was performed at 37 °C under the following conditions: The amount of OPD (concentrations within the range 0.125–1.25 × 10−3 mol L−1) was varied while keeping the amount of Cu-CDs fixed at 5 × 10−5 mol L−1 and H2O2 fixed at 1.25 × 10−3 mol L−1. The relation of reaction velocities and concentration of OPD was given.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of Cr(III) and Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: In the study, Cu-CDs were used as a peroxidase-mimicking to catalyze the oxidation of OPD. The general procedures for Cr(III) determination with the spectrofluorimetric method were as follows: Cr(III) ranges from 0 to 2.5 × 10−4 mol L−1 was added to a test tube, containing an appropriate volume of Cu-CDs solution (2 mg mL−1) and 0.1 mL H2O2 (5 × 10−2 mol L−1) and 0.2 mL OPD (5 × 10−3 mol L−1), then diluted to 4 mL with water. The solution was thoroughly mixed for a minimum waiting time of 30 s and then heated at 37 °C in water bath for 25 min. Afterwards, the fluorescence intensity of the system was measured at 564 nm, with excitation at 421 nm.

For the detection of Cr(VI), 0.2 mL of ABTS solution (5 × 10−3 mol L−1) as a reducing agent was added into the system with Cr(VI) and Cr(III) under the condition of room temperature. ABTS could quickly reduce Cr(VI) to Cr(III) and generate ABTS·+ under acidic conditions [37]. After the completely reduction of Cr(VI) by ABTS, the concentration of total Cr was measured according to the detection of Cr(III). After then, the concentration of Cr(VI) in environmental samples was subtracted Cr(III) from the total chromium.

XPS spectra were used to further study the chemical states of the Cu-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2a, full survey XPS spectra presented that only C, N, O and Cu were detected in the samples. The peak at 284.6 eV can be attributed to the signal from carbon in the instrument [38], and XPS spectra indicate the existence of C 1s, N 1s, Na, O 1s, Cu 2p. Fig. 2b shows the high-resolution XPS spectrum of the Cu-CDs in the Cu2p region. The XPS spectra of Cu 2p confirms the existence of Cu(II) and Cu(0), and the XPS spectra could be divided into three characteristic peaks of Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 (932.3 eV, 933.3 eV and 952.4 eV) [39].

To characterize the surface composition of the synthesized Cu-CDs, FTIR spectra in the range from 500 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1 was shown in Fig. 2c. The characteristic absorption band at 3423.98 cm−1 could be divided into -OH groups stretching vibration. The peak at 1631.08 cm−1 was attributed to -C=O stretching vibration, and those at 1399.09 cm−1 was assigned to -COOH on the surface of Cu-CDs. Moreover, the successful synthesis of Cu-CDs was verified by the stretching vibration of Cu-O at 527.36 cm−1. The small change in intensity in all vibrational modes for the peaks of CDs as shown in Fig. 2d. These results are in close agreement with similar observation reported in literature [40,41]. The morphology and particle size of the prepared Cu-CDs were studied by TEM. A TEM image of the Cu-CDs is shown in Fig. 2e. The TEM clearly reveals shape of these nanoparticles is close to spherical, with an average size of 5 nm. And the image showed that the Cu-CDs exhibits a good crystallinity (270 °C) with a lattice spacing of 0.23 nm, which was assigned to the crystal planes of (100) graphite. These results showed that the Cu-CDs has been successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic oxidation properties of Cu-CDs and Michaelis equations
ce:section.ce:para: The study investigated the catalytic reaction rate of Cu-CDs, the effect of pH and temperature for catalytic oxidation properties of Cu-CDs with the aim of boosting the production of high fluorescent DAP. Simultaneously, the relationship between reaction velocities and OPD concentration was then established. The temperature effect of the reaction on the catalytic efficiency was investigated with the above procedure. As shown in Fig. 3a, the catalytic efficiency reached the maximum at 37 °C (pH = 7), and the maximum fluorescence intensity of reaction system gradually decreased with the increasing of temperature. The result indicated that the Cu-CDs exhibited the best catalytic efficiency when the temperature of reaction reached 37 °C. The influence of pH for the system was investigated over the pH range 5–10 using 1 × 10−3 mol L−1 NaOH and 1 × 10−3 mol L−1 HCl. The data shown in Fig. 3b, fluorescence intensity was not obviously enhanced when pH changed. On the contrary, both of the maximum fluorescence intensity reduced with the increasing or decreasing of pH (pH ≠ 7). When the pH value was fixed at 7, the Cu-CDs exhibited the optimal catalytic activity for the oxidation reaction of OPD. Moreover, when the pH was below 7, the fluorescence intensity was dramatically decreased, which demonstrated that acid condition has a great effect on the catalytic oxidation reaction of OPD. It may be due to the existence of excess functions groups contained oxygen and nitrogen on the surface of Cu-CDs, such as, -OH, -COOH, -NH4. Under the condition of alkalinity, the interaction between these functional groups and OH− was enhanced [42]. At the same time, the catalytic performance of Cu-CDs was also weakened.

In order to achieve a reasonably experimental time tables, 25 min was chosen as the optimum reaction time. Therefore, the reaction temperature of 37 °C, optimum pH of 7, and reaction time of 25 min were selected for further studies.

As shown in Fig. 4 , the concentration of H2O2 was controlled in 1.25 × 10−3 mol L−1, and the concentration of OPD was regularly changed. The effect of OPD concentration was studied within the range of 0.125–1.25 × 10−3 mol L−1 for reaction rate. The kinetic parameters of the catalytic oxidation reaction were measured. And the catalytic reaction of Cu-CDs is in a specific substrate concentration wide, which is consistent with Michaelis kinetics model. Fig. 4 shows that the plots of initial reaction velocities (V0) versus the concentration of OPD, which showed the reaction velocities increased with the increasing of the concentration of OPD and then appeared an equilibrium at [OPD] = 1.25 × 10−3 mol L−1. The initial velocities upon corresponding the formation of DAP was obtained and plotted following Michaelis–Menten and Lineweaver–Burk equations. The Michaelis–Menten constant and the maximum velocities of reaction were determined to be 2.36 × 10−4 mol L−1 and 2.849 × 10−7 M·s−1. The result suggests a pseudo first order dependence on the substrate concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of the method
ce:section.ce:para: Apparently, the fluorescence intensity of oxidation reaction system was effectively reduced by Cr(III). In order to research the effect of the different Cr(III) concentration on the catalytic oxidation of the OPD, Cr(III) concentration was changed regularly. The result was shown in Fig. 5a, and the fluorescence intensity of the OPD system was decreased gradually increasing the concentrations of Cr(III) from 0.00 to 1.5 × 10−4 mol L−1. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity at 564 nm decreased with the increase of Cr(III), and the change of fluorescence intensity showed a good linearity (Fig. 5b).

In order to test the anti-interference performance of this method, the responses of fluorescence spectra were examined for various metal ions, such as, Hg2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, K+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Al3+ and Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 6a, the interference of metal ion species to 1 × 10−4 mol L−1 Cr3+ under the optimum reaction condition was examined. To verify the selective and sensitivity of the method, all of the ions were allowed at low concentrations (5 × 10−3 mol L−1). Fig. 6a shows that Cr(III) induced a prominent fluorescence intensity reduction, which makes the method as a highly sensitivity and selective fluorescent probes for Cr(III). Effect of metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of DAP in the presence and absence of Cr(III) and their mixture shown in Fig. 6b. Cu2+ had mild interference and should be add masking reagents (DDTC) to shelter the Cu2+ (Fig. 6c). Therefore, it can be concluded that the method can be used for selective detection of Cr(III).

The new spectrofluorimetric method for the simple and selective determination of Cr(III) and Cr(VI) using DAP as a fluorescent probe was developed. With the optimum condition described, the fluorescence intensity of DAP at 564 nm can be remarkably decreased by Cr(III). Fig. 7a shows that the aqueous solution of Cr(III) has single UV–Vis absorption peaks at 400 nm. The fluorescence spectra of DAP showed an intense fluorescence emission band at 564 nm under the maximum excitation wavelength of 421 nm. A biggish overlap between the UV–Vis absorption peaks of Cr(III) and the fluorescence excitation of DAP implies that the excitation wavelength of DAP can be absorbed by Cr(III), efficiently quenching the fluorescence emission of DAP through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and/or the IFE [43]. To further examine the rational quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetimes of Cu-CDs were investigated and shown in Fig. 7b. The lifetime values of Cu-CDs without and with Cr(III) were 7.44 ns and 7.23 ns. The results revealed that Cr(III) has slight impact on the decrease degree of fluorescence lifetimes of Cu-CDs. Therefore, the detection mechanism of Cr(III) mainly originates from IFE.

The method was used to the determination of Cr(III) and total Cr in the two certified reference materials for environmental samples. The aims of the determination were to evaluate the reliability of the method, and the result shown in Table 1 . In order to determine the total amount of Cr in the environmental water sample, different concentrations of Cr(III) and Cr(VI) were added into three environmental water samples, which were collected from various sources. The concentration of Cr(III) and Cr(VI) was measured according to the detection procedure of Cr(III) as described in Section 2.4. The results were shown in Table 2 , the recovery of the determination method for Cr(III) and Cr(VI) in environmental water samples from 97% to 102%. These results indicated that the present sensor could be applied to detection of Cr(VI) and Cr(III) in environmental samples.

In conclusion, Cu-CDs synthesized by using one-step pyrolysis are able to catalyze the oxidized reaction of OPD. DAP was found to be the fluorescent product of the reaction system. A decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed with the increase of Cr(III) in the DAP solution. Moreover, the experimental results reveal that the fluorescence quenching of DAP is attributed to the inner filter effect (IFE) between DAP and Cr(III). The ABTS was used as a reducing agent to rapidly reduce Cr(VI) to Cr(III). Based on those results, the facile fluorescent sensor can be used to detect Cr(VI) and Cr(III) species in environmental samples. However, it is not applicable for analyzing Cr under acidic conditions. Therefore, the method should be more comprehensively evaluated in the practical application.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: To the best of our knowledge, the authors have no conflict of interest, financial or otherwise.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was strongly supported by Kunming University of Science and Technology.
Qiulan Li: experiments, research, data curation, writing - original draft article.
Dezhi Yang: research, data analysis.
Yaling Yang: writing and editing.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chromium contamination accident in China: viewing environment policy of China

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Gao, J. Xia, Chromium contamination accident in China: viewing environment policy of China, Environ Sci Technol. 45 (2011) 8605-8606.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Li, G. Ji, J. Hu, S. Hu, X. Yuan, Remediation strategy and electrochemistry flushing & reduction technology for real Cr(VI)-contaminated soils, Chem Eng J. 334 (2018) 1281-1288.
